title: Descriptive Analysis of Approved and Rejected Grants in Russia, 1994-2016
text:
Intro & Data
I analyze data on grants from the Russian Foundation for Basic Research (RFBR). I have scrapped over 400,000 grant applications (accepted and rejected) from 1994 to 2016 from the RFBR website. The RFBR financially sponsors conferences, research, equipment, publishing projects, etc. There are eight main research areas of RFBR grants. From Table 1, we can see that by the number of grant applications, the first two places are taken by Biology & Medical Sciences and Physics & Astronomy: 21% and 20% of all grant applications for 1995-2016. In general, the majority of applications in STEM fields. Humanities & Social Sciences had 6.4% of applications.

Table 1: Number of grant applications by field

I made a gender determination based on the gender-specific endings of last names for 80% of the applicants (male-specific: “-ов”, “-ий”, “-ин”, “-ев”, “-ый”; female-specific: “-ва”, “-ая”, “-на”). Thus, we identified the gender of 326,661 grant applicants, 75.5% were male, and 24.5% were female.

      male 
    246728 (60.2%) 
  
  
      unknown 
    83054 (20.3%)

title: Descriptive Analysis of Approved and Rejected Grants in Russia, 1994-2016
text:
Results
In Figure 1, we see that the number of grant applications (accepted and rejected) ranged from a maximum in 1996 (26,518 applications) to a minimum in 2000 (12,830 applications). At the same time, the acceptance rate throughout the entire period was below 50% (the exception is 2001, with 51.7% approved applications).

Fig 1: Number of grant applications by year






Figure 2 shows that the number of applications submitted by women is always lower than that of men. Women submitted fewer than 5,000 applications annually (exceptions are in 2014 and 2016). Men averaged 10,727 applications per year from 1994-2016. That said, the proportion of female applications has steadily increased over the period under review. In 2016, the proportion of female applications was 31.8%; in 1994, it was 17.2%.


Fig 2: Number of grant applications by year and gender






Fig. 3 shows that Engineering as a separate field appeared in 2005. It may have been separated from Physics & Astronomy, and Earth Sciences, which show a gradual decline in the share of grant applications. We also see an increase in the share of applications in IT. Perhaps here, too, there is a redistribution from Math & Computer Science, as this subarea also shows a decrease in the share. Biology & Medical Sciences, Chemistry, and Humanities & Social Sciences have a stable share for 1994-2016.


Fig 3: Grants by research fields






In Fig. 4, we see that the proportion of female applications is consistently rising in all science fields. The highest proportion of female applications is in the Humanities & Social Sciences. At the end of the period, it was around 50%, and at the beginning of the period – in the 90s – it was around 30%. Similar dynamics in Biology & Medical Sciences – 30% of female applications at the start of the period and gender parity at the end. The lowest share of female applications in Physics & Astronomy, but even there, we see an increase - the lowest proportion in 1994 - 7%, and the highest in 2016 - 16.5%.


Fig 4: Share of women among grant applicants by fields









In Fig. 5, we see what proportion of applications are approved for women and men in different fields. For men, we see the highest share of won applications in Math & Computer Science (45.1% of applications submitted by men in that field). But only 37.2% of applications submitted by women won in Math & Computer Science (chisq test; p-value <0.001). In most fields women won a significantly fewer share of applications than men. At the same time, the highest gap we see in IT - men won 8.9 pp more applications than women. Humanities & Social Sciences in second place with an 8 pp gap. The lowest gap is in Physics & Astronomy – men won 1.4 pp more applications. There is only one area where the gap is not significant – Engineering. Here both women and men won the same share of applications.


Fig 5: Share of supported applications by gender





In Fig. 6, we see “nepotism” in favor of women in Engineering. The share of women who have won applications (of all applications by women in this field) was higher than the share of applications that men won. For example, in 2016, the gap was 4.46 pp in favor of women. The second area where we relatively often see “nepotism” in favor of women in different years is Physics & Astronomy. In all other areas, we see “nepotism” in favor of men, which is stable and reproduced yearly.


Fig 6: Difference in the share of supported grant applications between men and women

title: Descriptive Analysis of Approved and Rejected Grants in Russia, 1994-2016
text:
Conclusions and Research Direction

Most grants are in STEM fields. Humanities & Social Sciences represented 6.4% of all grant applications from 1994-2016 (Tab 1).
Only 24.5% of applicants are women (Tab 2). At the same time, we see a consistent increase in the share of female applications, from 17.2% to 31.8% over the period (Fig 2).
Gender parity in applications is achieved in two fields - Humanities & Social Sciences and Biology & Medicine (Fig 4). In all other areas, the proportion of female applications is significantly lower. However, there is a trend toward greater parity.
Men won applications significantly more often in all fields except Engineering. The highest “nepotism” in favor of men is in IT and Humanities & Social Sciences (Fig 5). The share of applications that men won is greater than the share of applications that women won not only in most fields but over most years. The exception is Engineering, Physics & Astronomy, where we see “nepotism” in favor of women by some years (Fig 6).

When I use the word nepotism, I mean the difference in the share of approved applications. We do not know the characteristics of applicants. Perhaps female applications are objectively worse in most fields for most years. So that is why the share of applications approved for men is higher. That is why I always put the word nepotism in quotes.
So we can distinguish three types of fields:

Physics & Astronomy, Engineering: relatively balanced fields regarding the prospects of female applications being approved. However, the proportion of female applications in these fields is still very low, although we see a gradual growth in the share of female applications.
Humanities & Social Sciences and Biology & Medicine and Medicine: the share of female applications here is comparable to the share of male applications. However, the share of applications approved for men is higher than the share of applications approved for women.
In all other fields, the share of female applications is low (although gradually increasing over the years), and men won applications relatively more often than women.

The possible research direction: we have information about the grant type – for young scientists, for conferences, for publishing books, etc. We could get more information from this variable, such as the maximum grant payment or the minimum requirements for applicants, such as degree, position, etc.
